html:
Repairing What Upstate Thunderstorm Season Leaves Behind

Spring and summer bring the county's most active severe weather, with hail and straight-line wind capable of doing real damage to a commercial roof in a matter of minutes. We assess storm damage systematically, section by section, rather than repairing only where the damage is visible from the ground.

How Hail and Wind Damage Different Roof Types

Hail impact bruises single-ply membranes, sometimes without breaking the surface, which means the damage isn't always obvious on a first pass. Those bruised spots can turn into leaks months later as the membrane weakens at the impact point under continued UV and thermal cycling.

Wind damage on a commercial low-slope roof tends to concentrate at the perimeter and corners, where uplift pressure is highest. Edge metal, coping details, and the outer few feet of membrane attachment take the brunt of a wind event, even when the interior field of the roof looks entirely untouched.

Rooftop mechanical units can also shift on their curbs during a significant wind event, cracking the seal at the base even if the unit itself stays in place. That's an easy failure point to miss during a ground-level walk since it requires getting up close to each curb to check.

Assessing Aged Roofs After a Storm

Mill-era buildings with older built-up roofs are more exposed to storm damage than newer systems, since brittle bitumen plies crack under hail and debris impact where a modern membrane might flex and hold. We inspect these roofs more closely after any significant weather event, since damage on an aged assembly is easier to miss without a close walk.

Falling limbs from mature trees near older properties around downtown Spartanburg and the surrounding mill villages are a recurring cause of localized punctures, particularly in the days after a wind event loosens dead branches that come down later.

We also check parapet coping and cap flashing more closely on older masonry buildings after a storm, since wind pressure works loose joints that were already weakened by decades of thermal cycling, well before the coping shows any visible sign of failure from the ground.

What a Storm Assessment Covers

  • Full roof walk documenting hail bruising and membrane punctures
  • Perimeter and corner check for wind uplift damage
  • Edge metal, coping, and flashing inspection
  • Drain and scupper check for storm debris blockage
  • Photo documentation organized by roof zone
  • Written repair scope with priority ranking

Repair Sequencing After a Significant Event

After a large storm affecting multiple properties across the county, response gets prioritized by active water intrusion first, then by risk of further damage if a section is left unrepaired. A punctured membrane over an active production area or racked inventory moves ahead of cosmetic damage on a lower-priority section of the same roof.

For distribution buildings along the I-85 corridor, we coordinate repair timing with dock schedules so crews aren't working directly over active loading operations during the busiest parts of the day.

Where a single storm affects multiple buildings in the same industrial park, we sequence work across the properties based on which roofs have the most exposed area rather than simply working in the order calls came in, so the facility with the greatest risk of continued water intrusion gets attention first.

Documenting Damage for Insurance and Warranty Review

Every storm assessment includes photo documentation organized by roof section, with damage measurements and repair recommendations formatted for an adjuster or manufacturer warranty review. We describe what we find factually and let the documentation support the claim rather than making promises about how a claim will be resolved.

Where a roof is still under manufacturer warranty, storm damage repairs are matched to the original system specification so the fix doesn't put existing warranty coverage at risk.

For facilities with multiple properties across the county, we can consolidate storm documentation into a single portfolio-level summary after a widespread event, which makes it easier for a risk management team to track claims across several buildings at once instead of piecing together separate files.

Storm Damage Questions Facilities and Property Teams Ask

How soon after a storm should a roof be assessed?

As soon as it's safe to access the roof. Hail bruising and wind damage aren't always visible from the ground, so an early assessment catches problems before they turn into leaks or spread to a wider area of the membrane.

Can hail damage a roof without creating a visible hole?

Yes. Hail often bruises a single-ply membrane without puncturing it, which weakens the material at the impact point even though the surface looks intact from a distance or on a quick walk-through.

Do you provide documentation for an insurance claim?

Yes, every assessment includes photo documentation and a written repair scope that supports a claim, though we don't make representations about how a claim will be resolved.

How long does storm damage repair typically take?

It depends on the extent of damage. Isolated punctures can be repaired in a single visit, while widespread wind or hail damage across a large roof, or damage affecting multiple rooftop units, takes longer to fully address and may be phased over several visits.

Will storm repair affect an existing roof warranty?

Not when the repair uses manufacturer-approved materials and methods. We check warranty status before starting any storm-related repair work, and where a claim is involved we coordinate the repair timeline with the manufacturer's requirements.
